(December 8, 2025)

Abstract

We use direct numerical simulations to investigate fluid-solid interactions in suspensions of rigid fibres settling under gravity in a quiescent fluid. The solid-to-fluid density ratio is 𝒪​(100)\mathcal{O}(100), while the Galileo number (G​aGa) and fibre concentration (n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}) are varied over the ranges G​a∈[180,900]Ga\in[180,900] and n​ℓf3∈[0.36,23.15]n\ell_{f}^{3}\in[0.36,23.15]; ℓf\ell_{f} denotes the fibre length and nn the number density.
At high G​aGa and/or low n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}, fibres cluster into gravity-aligned streamers with elevated concentrations and enhanced settling velocities, disrupting the flow homogeneity.
As G​aGa increases and/or n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} decreases, the fluid-phase kinetic energy rises and the energy spectrum broadens, reflecting enhanced small-scale activity. The flow anisotropy is assessed by decomposing the energy spectrum into components aligned with and transverse to gravity. Vertical fluctuations are primarily driven by fluid-solid interactions, while transverse ones are maintained by pressure-strain effects that promote isotropy.
With increasing G​aGa, nonlinear interactions become more prominent, producing a net forward energy cascade toward smaller scales, punctuated by localised backscatter events.
Analysis of the local velocity gradient tensor reveals distinct flow topologies: at low G​aGa, the flow is dominated by axisymmetric compression and two-dimensional straining; at high G​aGa, regions of high fibre concentration are governed by two-dimensional strain, while voids are associated with axisymmetric extension. The fluid motion is predominantly extensional rather than rotational.

keywords:
Fibre-laden flows, settling suspensions

1 Introduction

The sedimentation of solid objects, such as spherical particles and fibres, is a long-standing problem that has been extensively studied over the years (davis-acrivos-1985; guazzelli-morris-2011). It occurs in a wide range of natural phenomena, including sediment transport in rivers, the formation of raindrops, and the dispersion of atmospheric dust. Sedimentation also plays a crucial role in numerous industrial processes, where it is employed for liquid clarification, particle recovery, and the separation of materials based on density or size; it is also central to paper manufacturing. Despite its long history in both practical applications and fundamental research, many aspects of sedimenting suspensions remain poorly understood. The problem is inherently complex and depends on several key parameters, including the particle-to-fluid density ratio ρp/ρ\rho_{p}/\rho (where the subscript “pp” refers to a particle of generic shape), the Galileo number G​a=(|ρp/ρ−1|​ℓp3​g)1/2/νGa=(|\rho_{p}/\rho-1|\ell_{p}^{3}g)^{1/2}/\nu, which quantifies the ratio between buoyancy and viscous forces, and the volume fraction of the suspension ΦV=Vp/Vt\Phi_{V}=V_{p}/V_{t}, where VpV_{p} and VtV_{t} denote the volumes of the solid phase and the entire suspension, respectively. In the above, gg is the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration, ℓp\ell_{p} is the characteristic size of the particle, and ν\nu is the kinematic viscosity of the fluid.

In general, the dynamics of the carrier phase depends on the structure of the suspension and the spatial relative distribution of the particles. In turn, the spatial organisation of the suspension is strongly influenced by the dynamics of the fluid-phase fluctuations. The sedimentation of nonspherical particles, which is the focus of the present work, is an even more difficult task: the structure of the suspension and the organisation of the fluid velocity fluctuations also depend on the particles orientation.

1.1 Spherical particles

The monodisperse suspension of spherical particles is the most studied sedimentation system. In the absence of hydrodynamics interactions, i.e., in the extremely dilute case with ΦV→0\Phi_{V}\rightarrow 0, spherical particles sediment at the Stokes velocity 𝒖p=𝒖S​t≡(ρp−ρ)​ℓp2/(18​ν)​𝒈\bm{u}_{p}=\bm{u}_{St}\equiv(\rho_{p}-\rho)\ell_{p}^{2}/(18\nu)\bm{g} (batchelor-1967), where in this case ℓp\ell_{p} is the sphere diameter and 𝒈\bm{g} is the acceleration due to the gravity. As the suspension concentration ΦV\Phi_{V} increases, the backreaction of the solids on the fluid phase becomes significant, modulating the flow and affecting the settling velocity.
In semi-dilute and dense regimes, early studies (davis-acrivos-1985) showed that the mean settling velocity decreases due to the upward fluid flux induced by the displaced liquid. This effect is commonly described by a hindrance function, f​(ΦV)f(\Phi_{V}), such that the mean particle settling velocity satisfies ⟨𝒖p⟩p=f​(ΦV)​𝒖S​t\langle\bm{u}_{p}\rangle_{p}=f(\Phi_{V})\bm{u}_{St}, where ⟨⋅⟩p\langle\cdot\rangle_{p} denotes an average over the particles.
In non-dilute suspensions at sufficiently high Reynolds numbers, the structure is strongly influenced by wake interactions between particle pairs. Early studies examined flow past fixed spheres arranged in specific configurations to isolate collective effects from particle motion. tsuji-morikawa-terashima-1982 and tsuji-etal-2003 investigated pairs aligned with or transverse to the flow, while kim-elghobashi-sirignano-1993 and schouveiler-etal-2004 focused on side-by-side arrangements. These works consistently showed that the presence of a neighbouring sphere modifies the wake structure and its stability, affecting drag and lift forces depending on the relative positions. Additionally, flow past random arrays of fixed spheres over a broad Reynolds number range has been studied (e.g., hill-koch-ladd-2001; beetstra-vanDerHoef-kuipers-2007; tenneti-garg-subramaniam-2011), resulting in drag correlations dependent on suspension volume fraction and Reynolds number.

In recent years, several studies have investigated the sedimentation of particles in a quiescent fluid, using both experiments and direct numerical simulations (DNS) coupled with the immersed boundary method (IBM). Particular attention has been paid to how the microstructure of the suspension evolves across parameter space, and how these changes influence the average settling velocity.
One key contribution is that of uhlmann-doychev-2014, who provided a detailed description of particle motion in dilute suspensions with a volume fraction of ΦV=0.005\Phi_{V}=0.005 and a solid-to-fluid density ratio of ρp/ρ=1.5\rho_{p}/\rho=1.5. They considered two Galileo numbers, G​a=121Ga=121 and G​a=178Ga=178, to explore the influence of spatial particle distributions on collective dynamics. For the higher G​aGa, strong clustering was observed in the form of vertically elongated columnar structures. As a result of this clustering, the mean particle settling velocity increased by approximately 12% relative to the terminal velocity of an isolated sphere. This enhancement was attributed to particles preferentially sampling regions of downward fluid motion, which are generated by the clusters themselves.
zaidi-etal-2014 investigated the effects of particle Reynolds number and volume fraction on the settling behaviour of spherical particles. They varied the Reynolds number, based on the terminal velocity of an isolated particle, from R​e=1Re=1 to R​e=300Re=300, and the volume fraction from ΦV=0.005\Phi_{V}=0.005 to ΦV=0.05\Phi_{V}=0.05. At high R​eRe, particles exhibited strong clustering, primarily becoming entrained in regions of high fluid shear. At intermediate R​eRe, particle pairs were observed to form due to the presence of relatively weak wakes, which promote the draft–kissing–tumbling mechanism (fortes-etal-1987; wu-manasseh-1998).
huisman-etal-2016 experimentally investigated the effect of the Galileo number (110≤G​a≤310110\leq Ga\leq 310) on the settling of spherical particles in a quiescent fluid, at a fixed solid-to-fluid density ratio of ρp/ρ=2.5\rho_{p}/\rho=2.5 and a volume fraction of ΦV=𝒪​(10−3)\Phi_{V}=\mathcal{O}(10^{-3}). Consistent with the findings of uhlmann-doychev-2014, they used Voronoï tessellation to demonstrate that clustered particles preferentially align vertically and settle faster than isolated particles.
In a recent study, jiang-etal-2025 investigated the settling of spheroidal particles with a density ratio of ρp/ρ=2\rho_{p}/\rho=2 at a relatively low Galileo number, G​a=80Ga=80. They found that spherical particles do not cluster and settle with hindered velocity, whereas oblate and prolate particles form columnar clusters that enhance the mean settling speed. Additionally, they reported a k−3k^{-3} scaling in the fluid-phase energy spectrum (lance-bataille-1991), providing insight into the distinct mechanisms sustaining fluid velocity fluctuations.

The turbulence intensity of the surrounding fluid affects the settling velocity of particles. This effect was first identified by maxey-1987, who studied the settling of a single point particle in a homogeneous Gaussian random flow. He observed that inertial particles settle faster in turbulent flows than in quiescent fluids. The classical explanation attributes this enhancement to the preferential concentration of particles outside regions of high vorticity, causing them to sample predominantly downward-moving sides of vortices.
More recently, fornari-etal-2016 and fornari-etal-2016b investigated finite-size spherical particles settling in homogeneous isotropic turbulence at a Taylor microscale Reynolds number of R​eλ=u′​λ/ν=90Re_{\lambda}=u^{\prime}\lambda/\nu=90, where u′u^{\prime} is the root-mean-square velocity fluctuation. They considered particles slightly heavier than the fluid, with density ratios ρp/ρ⪆1\rho_{p}/\rho\gtrapprox 1, volume fractions in the range ΦV∈[0.005,0.01]\Phi_{V}\in[0.005,0.01], and Galileo numbers between 20≤G​a≤20020\leq Ga\leq 200. Their results showed that the average settling velocity decreases compared to quiescent conditions, with the reduction becoming more pronounced as the ratio of the particle’s isolated terminal velocity to the characteristic turbulent velocity fluctuation decreases. They attributed this reduction to non-stationary vortex shedding effects, which hinder the mean settling velocity in turbulent environments.

1.2 Non-spherical particles

The settling dynamics of non-spherical particles introduce additional complexities due to their anisotropic shapes and orientation-dependent interactions with the surrounding flow (voth-soldati-2017; chiarini-rosti-mazzino-2024). In the present study, we focus on fibre-like (or rod-like) rigid bodies, characterised by one dimension significantly larger than the others. Throughout, the subscript “ff” denotes fibre-related quantities.

Unlike spheres, the orientation of fibre-like particles strongly influences their settling behaviour in a quiescent fluid. In the absence of hydrodynamic interactions, the sedimentation velocity of a fibre with length ℓf\ell_{f}, diameter dfd_{f}, and density ρf\rho_{f} can be predicted by slender body theory as 𝒖f=Δ​ρ​df2​[A​(A)​𝒈+B​(A)​(𝒈⋅𝒑)​𝒑]\bm{u}_{f}=\Delta\rho d_{f}^{2}\left[A(\mathchoice{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}})\bm{g}+B(\mathchoice{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}})(\bm{g}\cdot\bm{p})\bm{p}\right], where A​(A)A(\mathchoice{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}) and B​(A)B(\mathchoice{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}) are coefficients that depend on the fibre aspect ratio A=ℓf/df\mathchoice{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}=\ell_{f}/d_{f}, 𝒑\bm{p} is the unit vector denoting the fibre orientation, and Δ​ρ=ρf−ρ\Delta\rho=\rho_{f}-\rho (batchelor-1970; mackaplow-shapfeh-1998).
It follows that the velocity components parallel, 𝒖f⋅𝒈/g\bm{u}_{f}\cdot\bm{g}/g, and perpendicular, 𝒖f−(𝒖f⋅𝒈)​𝒈/g2\bm{u}_{f}-(\bm{u}_{f}\cdot\bm{g})\bm{g}/g^{2}, to gravity depend explicitly on the fibre orientation. In suspensions of settling fibres, this implies that both the mean settling velocity and the structure of fluid-phase velocity fluctuations are strongly influenced by the orientations of the falling fibres.

The settling behaviour of fibres depends strongly on the effective particle volume fraction, commonly defined as n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}, where n=N/Vtn=N/V_{t} is the fibre number density (with NN the total number of fibres). This definition is motivated by the observation that fibres disturb the surrounding fluid over length scales on the order of their length ℓf\ell_{f}. Several studies, primarily experimental, have investigated how concentration influences the settling dynamics of rigid fibre suspensions.
For rigid fibres settling in Stokes flow, herzhaft-guazzelli-1999 observed that under dilute conditions, fibres tend to align with gravity and aggregate into vertically oriented clusters known as streamers, consistent with earlier findings (e.g., koch-shaqfeh-1989; herzhaft-etal-1996; mackaplow-shapfeh-1998). Notable contributions to the characterisation of streamers in fibre suspensions include those by metzger-guazzelli-butler-2005 and saintillan-shaqfeh-darve-2006. In this regime, the mean vertical sedimentation velocity is not hindered by the upward volume flux and exceeds the Stokes settling velocity of an isolated vertical fibre.
However, herzhaft-guazzelli-1999 also experimentally reported that at higher concentrations, although fibres still tend to orient vertically, the formation of streamers weakens and the average settling velocity decreases. This observation was later corroborated by simulations from butler-shaqfeh-2002, who showed that an initially homogeneous settling suspension may develop streamer structures surrounded by clarified fluid. They conjectured that this instability arises purely from hydrodynamic interactions coupling particle orientation and sedimentation rate within clusters. Furthermore, they confirmed that, depending on particle concentration and aspect ratio, streamers can enhance the sedimentation rate of the suspension beyond the maximum settling speed of an isolated fibre.
salmela-martinez-kataja-2005 experimentally measured the motion of individual fibres in settling suspensions across volume fractions ranging from ΦV=3.7×10−4\Phi_{V}=3.7\times 10^{-4} to approximately 0.01−0.40.01-0.4. Their study provided insights into how suspension concentration affects the average settling velocity. They observed that changes in settling velocity are accompanied by shifts in average fibre orientation: fibres preferentially orient horizontally in the dilute limit, while increasing concentration leads to alignment closer to the vertical.
More recently, banaei-etal-2020 conducted simulations of inertial settling for both rigid and flexible fibres. Fixing the Galileo number at G​a=160Ga=160 and varying the suspension concentration, they explored dilute to semi-dilute regimes. Their results showed that streamers form at high concentrations (n​ℓf3>10n\ell_{f}^{3}>10), but in agreement with herzhaft-guazzelli-1999, the streamers weaken as concentration increases due to reduced fibre clustering and mobility. Furthermore, they investigated the correlation between individual fibre velocities and the local suspension concentration at fibre locations, finding that the fastest settling fibres tend to reside in regions of higher concentration, whereas fibres exhibiting the fastest upward motion are predominantly found in areas with local concentrations below the average distribution value.

1.3 The present study

As discussed, much of the existing literature on fibre sedimentation in quiescent fluids has focused on the effects of G​aGa and n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} on the suspension microstructure and average settling velocity. However, the modulation of fluid-phase velocity fluctuations by settling fibres, and their dependence on these parameters, remains relatively unexplored. This study addresses this gap through DNS coupled with the IBM.
We consider a suspension of heavy rigid fibres with density ratio ρf/ρ=𝒪​(100)\rho_{f}/\rho=\mathcal{O}(100) settling under gravity in a quiescent fluid. The parameter space spans concentrations in the range n​ℓf3∈[0.36,23.15]n\ell_{f}^{3}\in[0.36,23.15], covering dilute to semi-dilute regimes, and Galileo numbers in the range G​a∈[180,900]Ga\in[180,900]. The lower bound of G​aGa aligns with recent work by banaei-etal-2020, while the upper bound extends beyond commonly studied values. The elevated G​aGa values emphasise buoyancy-driven effects and enhance fluid velocity fluctuations induced by the settling fibres.
The specific objectives of this study are:
(i) to investigate how G​aGa and n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} influence the organisation of fluid-phase velocity fluctuations induced by settling fibres;
(ii) to elucidate the mechanisms responsible for the generation and sustainment of these fluctuations under varying conditions;
(iii) to provide insight into the local structure of the fluid-phase velocity field; and
(iv) as a secondary aim, characterise the single-fibre statistics and collective spatial arrangement of settling fibres within the considered parameter range.

The remainder of the paper is organised as follows. §2 details the numerical methods and computational setup. The results are presented in §3, §4 and §5: §3 explores the collective fibre dynamics, while §4 and §5 analyse the sustaining mechanisms and local structure of the fluid-phase velocity fluctuations. Finally, concluding remarks and an outlook are provided in §6.

2 Methods

We study the motion of NN slender, rigid fibres settling under gravity in a Newtonian, incompressible fluid that is otherwise at rest. The fibres are modelled as one-dimensional slender objects, i.e., their length ℓf\ell_{f} is much larger than their diameter dfd_{f}, such that ℓf≫df\ell_{f}\gg d_{f}. Besides NN and ℓf\ell_{f}, the system is characterised by the fluid density ρ\rho, the kinematic fluid viscosity ν\nu, and the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration g=|𝒈|g=|\bm{g}|. The flow physics is entirely determined by the concentration of the suspension n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} (n=N/Vtn=N/V_{t} is the fibre number density with VtV_{t} the volume of the entire suspension), the density ratio ρf/ρ\rho_{f}/\rho, and the Galileo number G​aGa. As an alternative to using n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}, it is possible to look at the volume fraction of the suspension ΦV=Vf/Vt\Phi_{V}=V_{f}/V_{t}, where VfV_{f} denotes the volume occupied by the solid phase. The Galileo number can be written in the form of a Reynolds number, i.e. G​a=ug​ℓf/νGa=u_{g}\ell_{f}/\nu, where ugu_{g} is the gravitational velocity scale given by ug=(|ρf/ρ−1|​ℓf​g)1/2u_{g}=(|\rho_{f}/\rho-1|\ell_{f}g)^{1/2}.

The Dirac operator is numerically treated with the regularised function proposed by roma-peskin-berger-1999; in doing this, the diameter of the fibres is effectively around df=3​Δ​xd_{f}=3\Delta x, where Δ​x\Delta x is the grid spacing. Equation (2) is solved following the same approach of huang-etal-2007, the only difference being that the bending term is dealt implicitly (banaei-etal-2020). To prevent interpenetration between fibres, we use a fibre-to-fibre collision model, and we adopt the minimal collision model introduced by snook-guazzelli-butler-2012 by applying a constant forcing 𝑭c​o​l=𝑭0​𝒅^\bm{F}_{col}=\bm{F}_{0}\hat{\bm{d}} when the distance 𝒅\bm{d} between Lagrangian points of two distinct fibres is |𝒅|<Δc​o​l|\bm{d}|<\Delta_{col}; following olivieri-mazzino-rosti-2022, we set 𝑭0=1.0\bm{F}_{0}=1.0 and Δc​o​l=3​Δ​x\Delta_{col}=3\Delta x. A fixed-radius near-neighbors algorithm is used for the interaction between fibres to avoid an otherwise prohibitive computational cost (monti-etal-2021).

The fibres are released in a domain with periodic boundary conditions in all directions. Gravity acts in the vertical direction, aligned with the zz-axis, such that 𝒈=−g​e^z\bm{g}=-g\hat{\text{e}}_{z}. Due to the presence of gravity, the system does not reach a statistically steady state on its own. To compensate for the resulting net flow, a uniform body force is applied in the vertical direction to maintain zero mean flow in the periodic domain, following the approach of uhlmann-doychev-2014.

The computational domain has dimensions (Lx,Ly,Lz)=(3​π,3​π,6​π)(L_{x},L_{y},L_{z})=(3\pi,3\pi,6\pi), which is larger than that used in banaei-etal-2020 under similar conditions. The number of fibres is fixed at N=104N=10^{4}, and the fibre length is varied within the set ℓf∈{0.125​π, 0.25​π, 0.5​π}\ell_{f}\in\{0.125\pi,\,0.25\pi,\,0.5\pi\} to explore different concentrations. The corresponding values of the concentration n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} are 0.360.36, 2.892.89, and 23.1523.15. Since nn is kept constant across cases, longer fibres correspond to higher concentrations, while shorter fibres correspond to lower concentrations. For the intermediate concentration (n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89), we systematically vary the Galileo number within the set G​a∈{180,225,450,675,900}Ga\in\{180,225,450,675,900\}. For the lower concentration (n​ℓf3=0.36n\ell_{f}^{3}=0.36), we consider G​a∈{180,450}Ga\in\{180,450\}, while for the higher concentration (n​ℓf3=23.15n\ell_{f}^{3}=23.15), we explore G​a∈{180,450,900}Ga\in\{180,450,900\}. To focus on the generation of fluid-phase fluctuations, we consider heavy fibres with a density ratio ρf/ρ≈234\rho_{f}/\rho\approx 234, i.e. 𝒪​(102)\mathcal{O}(10^{2}).

The computational domain is discretised using (Nx,Ny,Nz)=(384,384,768)(N_{x},N_{y},N_{z})=(384,384,768) grid points in the three directions. This resolution ensures that Δ​x/⟨η⟩=𝒪​(1)\Delta x/\langle\eta\rangle=\mathcal{O}(1) in all cases, where ⟨η⟩=(ν3/ε)1/4\langle\eta\rangle=(\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4} is the average Kolmogorov length scale and ⟨ε⟩\langle\varepsilon\rangle is the average energy dissipation rate; ⟨⋅⟩\!\left\langle{\cdot}\right\rangle\! represents an average over realisations, time and along the three homogeneous directions. Specifically, the condition Δ​x/⟨η⟩<1\Delta x/\langle\eta\rangle<1 is satisfied for all simulations, except for the cases (n​ℓf3=0.36,G​a=450)(n\ell_{f}^{3}=0.36,\ Ga=450) and (n​ℓf3=2.89,G​a=900)(n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89,\ Ga=900), where Δ​x/⟨η⟩≈1.4\Delta x/\langle\eta\rangle\approx 1.4.
This choice results in a fixed fibre diameter of df=3​Δ​xd_{f}=3\Delta x across all cases.
Consequently, the fibre aspect ratio A=ℓf/df\mathchoice{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}=\ell_{f}/d_{f} varies with fibre length, taking values A=5.3\mathchoice{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}{\hbox{A}}=5.3, 10.710.7, and 21.321.3 for n​ℓf3=0.36n\ell_{f}^{3}=0.36, 2.892.89, and 23.1523.15, respectively. Each fibre is discretised using NLN_{L} Lagrangian points, selected such that the inter-point spacing Δ​s=ℓf/(NL−1)\Delta s=\ell_{f}/(N_{L}-1) approximately matches the Eulerian grid spacing Δ​x\Delta x. Accordingly, the number of Lagrangian points varies with fibre length: NL=17N_{L}=17, 3333, and 6565 for n​ℓf3=0.36n\ell_{f}^{3}=0.36, 2.892.89, and 23.1523.15, respectively. All simulations are advanced for a duration of 𝒪​(1000)​ℓf/ug\mathcal{O}(1000)\,\ell_{f}/u_{g} time units using a constant time step of Δ​t≈0.001​ℓf/ug\Delta t\approx 0.001\,\ell_{f}/u_{g}. The full set of simulation parameters is summarised in Table 1.
Data are collected after discarding the initial transient, once the system reaches a statistically steady state. After this point, flow statistics fluctuate around their mean values. All reported quantities and visualisations are robust and representative of the long-time dynamics. Data are sampled every 50​ℓf/ug50\ell_{f}/u_{g}.

3 Collective dynamics of the fibres

Figure 1: Settling velocity of the fibres. The filled symbols refer to the suspension of fibres with N=104N=10^{4}. The empty symbols refer to the terminal velocity of single fibres settling in the same conditions. These velocities are obtained by running additional simulations with the same grid resolution and same domain size, but with N=1N=1. Here and in the following, circles refer to cases where streamers are not formed or are rather weak, while squares refer to the cases where the streamers are evident and strong

Before discussing the organisation of the fluid-phase velocity fluctuations, we examine the formation of streamers and/or clusters at the considered parameters. Streamers are regions where fibres concentrate, correlating with high local settling velocities. Outside the streamers the local concentration of the suspension is low, and the fibres may also move in the direction opposite to gravity.

Figure 1 shows the average settling velocity of the fibres, ⟨wf⟩f\langle w_{f}\rangle_{f}, where the operator ⟨⋅⟩f\langle\cdot\rangle_{f} denotes an ensemble average over the NN fibres and in time. For comparison, the terminal velocity of a single fibre, wf,tw_{f,t}, is also shown (empty symbols), obtained from additional simulations with N=1N=1 under the same conditions. The error bars indicate the variance of the fibre settling velocity.
As shown in figure 1, fibres settle faster at lower concentrations, and increasing G​aGa enhances the magnitude of ⟨wf⟩f\langle w_{f}\rangle_{f}. In all cases considered, the average settling velocity exceeds the terminal velocity of an isolated fibre, i.e., |⟨wf⟩f|>|wf,t||\langle w_{f}\rangle_{f}|>|w_{f,t}|, consistent with the tendency of fibres to cluster and form regions of intensified downward motion.
A closer inspection reveals that the difference between ⟨wf⟩f\langle w_{f}\rangle_{f} and wf,tw_{f,t} is small at high concentrations and/or low G​aGa (n​ℓf3>2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}>2.89 or G​a≤450Ga\leq 450), but becomes more pronounced at lower concentrations and/or higher G​aGa (n​ℓf3≤2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}\leq 2.89 and/or G​a≥450Ga\geq 450). This behaviour is linked to the formation of columnar clusters or streamers by the settling fibres; see also herzhaft-guazzelli-1999; butler-shaqfeh-2002; metzger-guazzelli-butler-2005.

Figure 2 corresponds to n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89 and illustrates the dependence of streamer formation on G​aGa. A clear columnar accumulation of fibres is observed only for G​a≥450Ga\geq 450.
At higher G​aGa, the top-view visualisation reveals alternating regions: some where fibres are highly concentrated and settle with large negative velocities, and others that are nearly devoid of fibres. In the latter regions, the few fibres present occasionally exhibit upward motion.
Figure 3 illustrates the influence of n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} on streamer formation. In these visualisations, the fibre concentration varies while the Galileo number is fixed at G​a=180Ga=180 (top panels) and G​a=450Ga=450 (bottom panels). At both values of G​aGa, fibres form vertical columns for n​ℓf3=0.36n\ell_{f}^{3}=0.36 and n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89, but not for n​ℓf3=23.15n\ell_{f}^{3}=23.15.
More specifically, at G​a=180Ga=180, streamers are observed only at the lowest concentration (n​ℓf3=0.36n\ell_{f}^{3}=0.36), whereas at G​a=450Ga=450, they also emerge at the intermediate concentration (n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89). The absence of streamers at the highest concentration (n​ℓf3=23.15n\ell_{f}^{3}=23.15) is consistent with previous findings by herzhaft-guazzelli-1999 and banaei-etal-2020, and is attributed to the reduced and hindered mobility of the fibres under these conditions.

The influence of G​aGa and n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} on fibre clustering is illustrated in figure 4.
Figure 4(a)(a) shows that, once streamers form (G​a>225Ga>225), increasing the Galileo number enhances clustering.
Specifically, the probability of finding a pair of fibres at a distance r⪅3r\lessapprox 3 increases with G​aGa, while it decreases at larger separations. This trend reflects the development of void regions between streamers, as captured by the RDF, which satisfies g​(r)<1g(r)<1 for r⪆3r\gtrapprox 3.
Notably, for G​a≤225Ga\leq 225, i.e. in the absence of streamers, increasing G​aGa leads to a reduction in clustering. This non-monotonic behaviour is robust, as confirmed by the standard deviation of clustering metrics across different snapshots (not shown for brevity).
Such trends are consistent with previous observations in turbulent flows, where particle clustering is maximised at intermediate values of particle inertia or Stokes number, i.e., when inertia is neither negligible nor dominant (see, e.g. brandt-coletti-2022; chiarini-rosti-2024).
The effect of n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} on clustering is more subtle. On one hand, increasing n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} raises the volume fraction of the suspension, promoting greater clustering (see, e.g., olivieri-mazzino-rosti-2022). On the other hand, as visualised in figure 3, lower n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} values favour streamer formation, leading to locally higher concentrations. These competing effects result in the non-monotonic dependence of g​(r)g(r) on n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} shown in figure 4(b)(b). Nevertheless, the distributions of g​(r)g(r) and CC for n​ℓf3=0.36n\ell_{f}^{3}=0.36 consistently correspond to streamer formation at both G​a=180Ga=180 and G​a=450Ga=450.

As expected, the joint probability density function (JPDF) of CC and wfw_{f} exhibits a strong dependence on G​aGa. For low values of G​aGa (G​a≤225Ga\leq 225), i.e., in the absence of streamers, the JPDF is approximately symmetric with respect to inversion of both axes. A slight bias is nevertheless observed toward events where (wf−⟨wf⟩f)​(C−⟨C⟩f)<0(w_{f}-\langle w_{f}\rangle_{f})(C-\langle C\rangle_{f})<0.
For G​a≥450Ga\geq 450, this bias becomes more pronounced, with the distribution skewed toward the second and fourth quadrants, consistent with the formation of streamers. Interestingly, at G​a=450Ga=450, the distribution peak lies in the fourth quadrant, indicating a significant number of fibres settling more slowly than the average despite the presence of streamers; see the central panels in figure 2.
At even higher G​aGa (G​a≥675Ga\geq 675), the peak shifts to the second quadrant. In this regime, the probability of events with wf≥⟨wf⟩fw_{f}\geq\langle w_{f}\rangle_{f} and C<⟨C⟩fC<\langle C\rangle_{f} diminishes, suggesting that most fibres are tightly packed within the streamers and few remain in low-concentration regions.

This section concludes with an analysis of the fibres settling angle. We define the settling angle ϕ\phi such that cos⁡(ϕ)=𝒆^z⋅ℓf/ℓf\cos(\phi)=\hat{\bm{e}}_{z}\cdot\bm{\ell}_{f}/\ell_{f}, where ℓf\bm{\ell}_{f} is the vector that connects the free ends of the fibres; cos⁡(ϕ)=1\cos(\phi)=1 (cos⁡(ϕ)=0\cos(\phi)=0) indicates fibres that settle aligned with (perpendicular to) the gravity. Figure 6 shows that at the present parameters, fibres tend to align with the direction of the gravity, in agreement with the experimental results of previous authors (see for example herzhaft-guazzelli-1999). Figure 6(a)(a) details the influence of G​aGa on the distribution of cos⁡(ϕ)\cos(\phi) by fixing n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89, while figure 6(b)(b) characterises the influence of n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} by fixing G​a=180Ga=180. The influence of G​aGa on cos⁡(ϕ)\cos(\phi) is not monotonous. When increasing the Galileo number from G​a=180Ga=180 to G​a=450Ga=450 the probability of events with cos⁡(ϕ)=1\cos(\phi)=1 increases and then decreases for larger G​aGa, being minimum for G​a=900Ga=900. Arguably, this is because once the streamers form and G​aGa is large enough, the backreaction of the chaotic fluid phase on the fibre motion becomes relevant (see §4), tending to restore a more uniform distribution. Turning to figure 6(b)(b), the data show that, for fixed G​aGa, fibres tend to align more strongly with the direction of gravity as the concentration increases.
We now shift our focus to the fluid phase. The objectives of this section are: (i) to examine the influence of G​aGa and n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} on the scale-by-scale distribution of kinetic energy, and (ii) to elucidate the mechanisms responsible for the generation of fluid-phase velocity fluctuations. We first analyse integral flow quantities in §4.1, then investigate the energy distribution across scales in §4.2, and finally discuss the mechanisms sustaining velocity fluctuations in §4.3.

Figure 7(a)(a) shows the dependence of the fluid-phase kinetic energy, ⟨E⟩\langle E\rangle, on G​aGa and n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}. The fluid-phase kinetic energy increases with decreasing n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} and increasing G​aGa, and exhibits a plateau for G​a⪆450Ga\gtrapprox 450.
At lower n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} and higher G​aGa, fibres settle with larger vertical velocities (see figure 1), generating stronger momentum deficits in the fluid phase. This enhances the production of fluid-phase velocity fluctuations (see §4.2).
Specifically, we observe that the normalised dissipation rate, ⟨ε⟩​ℓf/ug3\langle\varepsilon\rangle\ell_{f}/u_{g}^{3}, follows a power-law increase with the Galileo number, i.e., ⟨ε⟩​ℓf/ug3∼G​aαε\langle\varepsilon\rangle\ell_{f}/u_{g}^{3}\sim Ga^{\alpha_{\varepsilon}}, where αε≈1/2\alpha_{\varepsilon}\approx 1/2. The increased production of velocity fluctuations at higher G​aGa leads to enhanced viscous dissipation. This scaling implies ⟨ε⟩∼ug7/2​ℓf−1/2​ν−1/2\langle\varepsilon\rangle\sim u_{g}^{7/2}\ell_{f}^{-1/2}\nu^{-1/2}, as shown in figure 7(d)(d).
In contrast, the average Kolmogorov length scale decreases with increasing G​aGa, exhibiting a power-law behaviour ⟨η⟩/ℓf∼G​a−αη\langle\eta\rangle/\ell_{f}\sim Ga^{-\alpha_{\eta}}, with αη≈1\alpha_{\eta}\approx 1. This G​a−1Ga^{-1} scaling implies ⟨η⟩/ℓf∼G​a−1∼(ug​ℓf/ν)−1∼ν/(ug​ℓf)\langle\eta\rangle/\ell_{f}\sim Ga^{-1}\sim\left(u_{g}\ell_{f}/\nu\right)^{-1}\sim\nu/(u_{g}\ell_{f}), and thus ⟨η⟩∼ν/ug\langle\eta\rangle\sim\nu/u_{g}, as illustrated in figure 7(e)(e).
This suggests that at the present parameters the smallest flow scales are independent of the fibre length ℓf\ell_{f}, and instead depend on the fibre diameter dfd_{f}, which is held constant across all cases (see §2). This finding is consistent with the observation that fibres tend to align with gravity (see figure 6), implying that the characteristic scale of the velocity fluctuations around the fibres is set by their effective frontal area (∼df2\sim d_{f}^{2}).

We now investigate the scale-by-scale energy content of the fluid phase, i.e., the fluid energy spectrum. To properly account for the flow anisotropy, we consider scales/wavenumbers in the zz directions, r⟂r_{\perp} and κ⟂\kappa_{\perp}, and scales/wavenumbers in the x−yx-y plane, r∥r_{\parallel} and κ∥\kappa_{\parallel}, separately. Following pope-2000, we define the velocity-spectrum tensor Φi​j​(𝜿)\Phi_{ij}(\bm{\kappa}) as
ℰi​j​(κ∥,κ⟂)=∬−∞+∞Φi​j​(𝜿)​δ​(|𝜿2​D|−κ∥)​d​𝜿2​D.\mathcal{E}_{ij}(\kappa_{\parallel},\kappa_{\perp})=\iint\limits_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\Phi_{ij}(\bm{\kappa})\delta(|\bm{\kappa}_{2D}|-\kappa_{\parallel})\text{d}\bm{\kappa}_{2D}.
Here, 𝜿2​D≡(κx,κy,0)\bm{\kappa}_{2D}\equiv(\kappa_{x},\kappa_{y},0), κ∥≡κx2+κy2\kappa_{\parallel}\equiv\sqrt{\kappa_{x}^{2}+\kappa_{y}^{2}}, and κ⟂≡κz\kappa_{\perp}\equiv\kappa_{z}; the operator ⋅^\hat{\cdot} denotes the Fourier transform of a physical quantity, and the superscript “∗*” indicates complex conjugation.
To properly account for flow anisotropy, we define ℰ1​c≡ℰz​z\mathcal{E}_{1c}\equiv\mathcal{E}_{zz} and ℰ2​c≡ℰx​x+ℰy​y\mathcal{E}_{2c}\equiv\mathcal{E}_{xx}+\mathcal{E}_{yy}. The total energy spectrum is then given by ℰ3​c=ℰ1​c+ℰ2​c\mathcal{E}_{3c}=\mathcal{E}_{1c}+\mathcal{E}_{2c}.

Figure 9 presents the dependence of the total energy spectra, ℰ3​c∥\mathcal{E}_{3c}^{\parallel} (a,c) and ℰ3​c⟂\mathcal{E}_{3c}^{\perp} (b,d), on G​aGa (a,b) and fibre concentration n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} (c,d). The spectra are normalised by the total kinetic energy, such that ∫0∞ℰ3​c∥,⟂/⟨E⟩​d​κ∥,⟂=1\int_{0}^{\infty}\mathcal{E}_{3c}^{\parallel,\perp}/\langle E\rangle\text{d}\kappa_{\parallel,\perp}=1; this allows us to examine the relative scale-by-scale distribution of energy across cases.
These trends are especially apparent in ℰ3​c∥\mathcal{E}_{3c}^{\parallel} (a), where the suppression of energy for κ⪅κℓ\kappa\lessapprox\kappa_{\ell} and the enhancement for κ⪆κℓ\kappa\gtrapprox\kappa_{\ell} is more pronounced; here, κℓ=2​π/ℓf\kappa_{\ell}=2\pi/\ell_{f} denotes the wavenumber associated with the fibre length.
This energy redistribution is consistent with the spectral shortcut mechanism (brandt-coletti-2022), previously observed for spherical particles (tenCate-etal-2004) and rigid/flexible fibres in turbulence (olivieri-mazzino-rosti-2022; cannon-olivieri-rosti-2024). The fibres affect the fluid in two main ways: (i) their settling induces a local momentum deficit, generating velocity fluctuations across a broad range of scales, and (ii) they disrupt large-scale structures (κ⪅κℓ\kappa\lessapprox\kappa_{\ell}), transferring energy to smaller scales (κ⪆κℓ\kappa\gtrapprox\kappa_{\ell}). Both effects intensify with increasing G​aGa, in line with the rise in total energy (figure 7) and the enhanced small-scale energy content.
At larger scales, the spectra approach a Kolmogorov-like κ−5/3\kappa^{-5/3} scaling, particularly in ℰ3​c⟂\mathcal{E}_{3c}^{\perp} and at lower G​aGa, where large-scale energy depletion is less pronounced. At higher wavenumbers, the spectra steepen, indicating a dissipative range.
Varying the concentration n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} produces qualitatively similar effects: decreasing n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3} leads to stronger scale separation, reduced energy at large scales, and enhanced energy at small scales. These observations are consistent with earlier results in figures 7 and 8.

The degree of anisotropy varies across scales. At low wavenumbers, ℰ2​c∥\mathcal{E}_{2c}^{\parallel} flattens, suggesting weak correlations in large-scale horizontal motions. Figure 14 shows the two-dimensional distributions of the budget terms ℱi​c\mathcal{F}_{ic}, 𝒫i​c\mathcal{P}_{ic}, and 𝒟i​c\mathcal{D}_{ic} (i=1,2i=1,2) in the (κ∥,κ⟂)(\kappa_{\parallel},\kappa_{\perp}) space. For brevity, only the case with G​a=180Ga=180 and n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89 is reported, though similar trends are observed across all cases.
The maps highlight pronounced flow anisotropy at large scales: all terms peak near κ⟂→0\kappa_{\perp}\to 0 over a broad range of κ∥\kappa_{\parallel}, and decay more rapidly with increasing κ⟂\kappa_{\perp} than with κ∥\kappa_{\parallel}. This anisotropic distribution is consistent with the ℰ1​c\mathcal{E}_{1c} and ℰ2​c\mathcal{E}_{2c} spectra. At smaller scales, where κ⟂≈κ∥\kappa_{\perp}\approx\kappa_{\parallel}, the distributions become more isotropic.
Focusing on the ℰ1​c\mathcal{E}_{1c} budget (left panels), the fluid-solid coupling term ℱ1​c\mathcal{F}_{1c} is positive across all wavenumbers, acting as a source of vertical velocity fluctuations. Its peak at low κ∥\kappa_{\parallel} and κ⟂→0\kappa_{\perp}\to 0 reflects the characteristic scales of the streamers. This input is primarily balanced by viscous dissipation 𝒟1​c\mathcal{D}_{1c}, except at low wavenumbers (κ∥,κ⟂⪅κℓ\kappa_{\parallel},\kappa_{\perp}\lessapprox\kappa_{\ell}), where the negative pressure-strain term 𝒫1​c\mathcal{P}_{1c} also contributes significantly. Although smaller in magnitude, 𝒫1​c<0\mathcal{P}_{1c}<0 persists down to the smallest scales, particularly where κ⟂≈κ∥\kappa_{\perp}\approx\kappa_{\parallel}, indicating that pressure plays a consistent role in promoting isotropy across scales.
By contrast, the ℰ2​c\mathcal{E}_{2c} budget (right panels) reveals a different balance. Here, the in-plane velocity fluctuations are primarily sustained by the pressure-strain term 𝒫2​c=−𝒫1​c>0\mathcal{P}_{2c}=-\mathcal{P}_{1c}>0, which remains positive throughout. The coupling term ℱ2​c\mathcal{F}_{2c} is negative at large scales (especially where κ⟂≈κ∥\kappa_{\perp}\approx\kappa_{\parallel}), suggesting that fibres extract energy from the in-plane motions. At smaller scales (κ⟂,κ∥⪆κℓ\kappa_{\perp},\kappa_{\parallel}\gtrapprox\kappa_{\ell}), however, ℱ2​c\mathcal{F}_{2c} becomes positive, indicating a reversal where fibres begin to energise the flow. The large-scale negative ℱ2​c\mathcal{F}_{2c} is balanced primarily by 𝒫2​c\mathcal{P}_{2c}, particularly at low wavenumbers and intermediate scales. Conversely, at small κ⟂\kappa_{\perp} and large κ∥\kappa_{\parallel}, the positive ℱ2​c\mathcal{F}_{2c} is balanced by viscous dissipation 𝒟2​c\mathcal{D}_{2c}.

We now move to the nonlinear term 𝒩i​c\mathcal{N}_{ic}. Figure 12 shows that when the intensity of the fluctuations is low, 𝒩i​c∥,⟂\mathcal{N}_{ic}^{\parallel,\perp} is rather small meaning that the inter-scale energy transfer is substantially negligible and that the fluctuations are mainly sustained by local mechanisms. However, figure 13 shows that the nonlinear term becomes relevant as G​aGa increases, in agreement with the increasing fluid inertia. Interestingly, the in-plane and out-of-plane terms 𝒩ic,∥∥,⟂\mathcal{N}_{ic,\parallel}^{\parallel,\perp} and 𝒩i​c,⟂∥,⟂\mathcal{N}_{ic,\perp}^{\parallel,\perp} behave differently. The 𝒩ic,∥∥,⟂\mathcal{N}_{ic,\parallel}^{\parallel,\perp} term is a sink at small scales and a source at large scales, denoting an average energy transfer from smaller to larger scales. The 𝒩i​c,⟂∥,⟂\mathcal{N}_{ic,\perp}^{\parallel,\perp} term, instead, exhibits a more classical behaviour. It is a sink at the large scales and a source at the smaller scales, denoting thus an average energy transfer from larger to smaller scales. Nonetheless, when examining the total scale-by-scale energy transfer rate 𝒩3​c∥,⟂\mathcal{N}_{3c}^{\parallel,\perp}, we observe that |𝒩3​c,⟂∥,⟂|>|𝒩3c,∥∥,⟂||\mathcal{N}_{3c,\perp}^{\parallel,\perp}|>|\mathcal{N}_{3c,\parallel}^{\parallel,\perp}| across almost all wavenumbers. The overall energy transfer is directed from large to small scales, as 𝒩3​c∥,⟂<0\mathcal{N}_{3c}^{\parallel,\perp}<0 at small κ\kappa and 𝒩3​c∥,⟂>0\mathcal{N}_{3c}^{\parallel,\perp}>0 at large κ\kappa.

The average picture of the interscale exchanges is not the complete physical process: although the average picture may predict a transfer from larger to smaller scales, there may exist events localised in space and time where the opposite occurs. To address this aspect in the present context, we analyse the probability distribution functions of the energy transfer rate at different scales. We resort on the real-space counterpart of equation (12), i.e., the Kármán-Howarth-Monin-Hill (KHMH) equation, also known as the generalised Kolmogorov equation (danaila-etal-2001; hill-2002). The KHMH equation is the budget equation for the velocity structure function ⟨δ​q2⟩=⟨δ​ui​δ​ui⟩\langle\delta q^{2}\rangle=\langle\delta u_{i}\delta u_{i}\rangle, where δ​ui\delta u_{i} is the velocity increment between two points 𝒙\bm{x} and 𝒙′\bm{x}^{\prime} identified using their mid-point 𝑿=(𝒙+𝒙′)/2\bm{X}=(\bm{x}+\bm{x}^{\prime})/2 and their separation vector 𝒓=𝒙′−𝒙\bm{r}=\bm{x}^{\prime}-\bm{x}, i.e. δ​ui​(𝑿,r,t)=ui​(𝑿+𝒓/2,t)−ui​(𝑿−𝒓/2,t)\delta u_{i}(\bm{X},r,t)=u_{i}(\bm{X}+\bm{r}/2,t)-u_{i}(\bm{X}-\bm{r}/2,t). Since the present flow features three homogeneous directions and is statistically stationary, ⟨δ​q2⟩\!\left\langle{\delta q^{2}}\right\rangle\! depends only on 𝒓=(rx,ry,rz)\bm{r}=(r_{x},r_{y},r_{z}). To get rid of the directional information we also integrate in the 𝒓\bm{r} space over spherical shells of radius r=|𝒓|r=|\bm{r}|, with surface S​(r)S(r) and volume V​(r)V(r). In this homogeneous and isotropic framework, the KHMH equation reads:

1S​(r)​∫S​(r)⟨δ​uj​δ​q2⟩​nj​d​Σ⏟⟨Φ⟩​(r)=1S​(r)​∫S​(r)2G​a​∂⟨δ​q2⟩∂rj​nj​d​Σ⏟⟨D⟩​(r)+−43​1G​a​⟨∂ui∂xj​∂ui∂xj⟩⏟⟨ε⟩​r+1S​(r)​∫V​(r)2​⟨δ​ui​δ​ff​s,i⟩​d​Ω⏟⟨F​(r)⟩.\begin{gathered}\underbrace{\frac{1}{S(r)}\int_{S(r)}\!\left\langle{\delta u_{j}\delta q^{2}}\right\rangle\!n_{j}\text{d}\Sigma}_{\!\left\langle{\Phi}\right\rangle\!(r)}=\underbrace{\frac{1}{S(r)}\int_{S(r)}\frac{2}{Ga}\frac{\partial\!\left\langle{\delta q^{2}}\right\rangle\!}{\partial r_{j}}n_{j}\text{d}\Sigma}_{\!\left\langle{D}\right\rangle\!(r)}+\\

Here, DD denotes the viscous diffusion, ε\varepsilon the dissipation rate, and FF the forcing associated with the fluid–structure interaction, and Φ\Phi, the quantity of interest, represents the nonlinear energy cascade rate at the considered scale rr; 𝒏\bm{n} is the outward-pointing unit normal to the spherical surface. In this convention, Φ>0\Phi>0 corresponds to backscatter, i.e., energy transfer from smaller to larger scales, whereas Φ<0\Phi<0 denotes a forward cascade, with energy transferred from larger to smaller scales. For a full derivation of the governing equations and additional details, the reader is referred to gatti-etal-2020.

Figure 15 plots the probability distribution function of Φ​(r)\Phi(r) for n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89 and different G​aGa. We consider three different separations: r/ℓf≈0.25,0.75r/\ell_{f}\approx 0.25,0.75, and 1.51.5. For all scales, the distribution shows long positive and negative tails, hinting at an intermittent interscale energy transfer, similar to what has been found in sustained turbulent flows (ishihara-gotoh-kaneda-2009). Backscatter and forward transfers coexist, with extreme events occurring with a higher probability than for a normal distribution. However, for all separations, the distributions are asymmetric and exhibit a longer negative tail. Accordingly, ⟨Φ⟩\langle\Phi\rangle is negative at all scales, and the average interscale energy transfer is from larger to smaller scales, like in classical homogeneous and isotropic turbulence (frisch-1995); this agrees with the Fourier-space investigation. In agreement with figures 12 and 13, when G​aGa increases and the fluid velocity fluctuations intensify, the tails of the distributions become wider (see the left panels) indicating that the inter-scale energy transfers become more relevant.

The fluxes normalised by their root-mean-square values (right panels of figure 15) highlight the influence of G​aGa on the intermittency of the energy transfer. As G​aGa increases, the tails of the normalised-flux distributions shrink across all separations, indicating a reduction in intermittency. This behaviour is linked to the nature of the velocity field. For lower G​aGa, the flow remains relatively smooth except in the immediate vicinity of the fibres, where sharp and intense fluctuations occur. This is illustrated in figure 16, which presents isosurfaces of the dissipation field. At low G​aGa, regions of high dissipation are confined near the fibre surfaces, where flow deformation is most intense. In this regime, the energy injected at small scales is dissipated almost immediately and locally. In contrast, at higher G​aGa, the influence of the fibres extends farther into the fluid domain, and the injected energy is dissipated over a broader region. This results in a smoother, less spatially intermittent dissipation field.

5 The local structure of the fluctuations

In this section, we characterise the local structure of the fluid-phase velocity fluctuations. We focus on the case with n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89 and consider increasing values of G​aGa, thereby spanning conditions from weak to strong velocity fluctuations. When the fluid is sufficiently smooth in the neighbourhood of a point (𝒙0,t)(\bm{x}_{0},t), and the spatial variation of the velocity gradient ai​j=∂uj/∂xia_{ij}=\partial u_{j}/\partial x_{i} is relatively small, the velocity field can be locally approximated by a first-order Taylor expansion: ui​(𝒙,t)=ui​(𝒙0,t)+ai​j​(𝒙0,t)​(xj−x0,j)+𝒪​(|𝒙−𝒙0|2)u_{i}(\bm{x},t)=u_{i}(\bm{x}_{0},t)+a_{ij}(\bm{x}_{0},t)(x_{j}-x_{0,j})+\mathcal{O}(|\bm{x}-\bm{x}_{0}|^{2}). The velocity gradient tensor ai​ja_{ij} encapsulates key information about the structure of the fluctuating field, as it: (i) reflects the dynamics at small scales (meneveau-2011), and (ii) is more sensitive to the non-Gaussian features of turbulence than the velocity field itself (tsinober-2000).

We decompose ai​ja_{ij} into its symmetric and anti-symmetric parts, i.e., the strain-rate tensor si​j=(ai​j+aj​i)/2s_{ij}=(a_{ij}+a_{ji})/2 and the rotation rate tensor wi​j=(ai​j−aj​i)/2w_{ij}=(a_{ij}-a_{ji})/2. The former is related to strain and dissipation, while the latter to vorticity and enstrophy. The velocity gradient field is completely characterised when knowing: (i) the three principal rates of strain α≥β≥γ\alpha\geq\beta\geq\gamma, i.e., the three eigenvalues of si​js_{ij}, (ii) the vorticity magnitude ω2=𝝎⋅𝝎\omega^{2}=\bm{\omega}\cdot\bm{\omega}, i.e., the enstrophy, and (iii) the orientation of 𝝎\bm{\omega} relative to the three principal axes of strain, i.e., the eigenvectors of si​js_{ij} (𝒆^α\hat{\bm{e}}_{\alpha}, 𝒆^β\hat{\bm{e}}_{\beta} and 𝒆^γ\hat{\bm{e}}_{\gamma}); see davidson-2004. Due to the incompressibility constraint α+β+γ=0\alpha+\beta+\gamma=0, meaning that α\alpha is always nonnegative, γ\gamma is always nonpositive, while β\beta has a sign that determines the local straining state. The si​js_{ij} and wi​jw_{ij} tensors are strongly related to vortex stretching and self-amplification of the strain, which are manifested in the net production of enstrophy ω2=wi​i\omega^{2}=w_{ii} and strain s2=si​is^{2}=s_{ii}.

5.1 Straining state and alignments

We start characterising α\alpha, β\beta and γ\gamma (see figure 17) by using s∗s^{*} (lund-rogers-1994), which is defined as:

s∗=3​6​α​β​γ(α2+β2+γ2)3/2.s^{*}=\frac{3\sqrt{6}\alpha\beta\gamma}{(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+\gamma^{2})^{3/2}}.

(22)

For a random velocity gradient field with no preferred structure, all states are equally probable, and s∗s^{*} has a uniform distribution. When s∗=1s^{*}=1, we have that α=β=−γ/2\alpha=\beta=-\gamma/2 meaning that the state of straining is an axisymmetric extension, in which a small element of fluid extends symmetrically in two directions and contracts in the third one. When s∗=−1s^{*}=-1, instead, γ=β=−α/2<0\gamma=\beta=-\alpha/2<0, and the state of straining corresponds to an axisymmetric compression, where the elements of fluid contract in two directions and extend in the third one. When s∗=0s^{*}=0, we have that β=0\beta=0 meaning that the straining state is two-dimensional, which is typical in shear-dominated regions (meneveau-2011).

Figure 17: Distribution of s∗s^{*} for n​ℓf3=2.89n\ell_{f}^{3}=2.89 and different G​aGa.

Figure 17 shows that the local state of straining changes with G​aGa. For small G​aGa, events with s∗≤0s^{*}\leq 0 are promoted and states with two directions of compression and one direction of extension are more frequent. At the intermediate G​a=225Ga=225 and G​a=450Ga=450, instead, the most likely value is s∗=0s^{*}=0: two-dimensional straining states with one direction of compression and one direction of extension are mainly favoured. At these G​aGa, indeed, the fluid-phase velocity fluctuations are rather weak and mainly localised in the vicinity of the settling fibres (see figure 16), where the flow is shear-dominated. Similar results were observed in fully developed turbulent flows laden with particles or fibres (schneiders-etal-2017; cannon-olivieri-rosti-2024; chiarini-tandurella-rosti-2025). A further increase of G​aGa leads to an enhancement of the s∗>0s^{*}>0 events, and for G​a=900Ga=900 the distribution peaks at s∗=1s^{*}=1. At the large G​aGa, the fluid inertia is not negligible (see §4.3) and the local structure of the fluctuations is more similar to that of homogeneous and isotropic turbulence (HIT) (lund-rogers-1994), i.e., dominated by axisymmetric extensions.

To account for the flow inhomogeneity induced by the presence of the streamers, figure 18 considers the s∗−ws^{*}-w JPDF. As mentioned above, w<0w<0 locates the flow regions with a large concentration of fibres. For small G​aGa (G​a≤450Ga\leq 450), the most likely events have w≈0w\approx 0 and s∗≤0s^{*}\leq 0. This is consistent with the observation that at these G​aGa the fluid-phase velocity fluctuations are mainly associated with the shear layers that form in the vicinity of the fibres, which are dominated by a two-dimensional straining state. For larger G​aGa the scenario changes due to the presence of the streamers, and the state of strain in the ascending (w>0w>0) and descending (w<0w<0) regions differs. In the ascending region the JPDF peaks at s∗=1s^{*}=1, while in the descending one at s∗≤0s^{*}\leq 0. The ascending stream is dominated by axisymmetric extensional events, reminiscent of HIT, while the descending stream is dominated by events characterised by a two-dimensional state of strain, like at lower G​aGa. At these values of G​aGa, two distinct mechanisms coexist in organising the fluid-phase velocity fluctuations (see §4.3). In the descending stream, where the fibre concentration is high, fluctuations are primarily driven by the fluid-solid interaction. In contrast, in the ascending stream, where the fibre concentration is low, the velocity fluctuations are also sustained by nonlinear mechanisms (see the following discussion).

Figure 19 shows the alignment between 𝝎\bm{\omega} and the principal axes of strain. These quantities are strongly related to the dynamics of the fluctuations: mechanisms such as vortex stretching, that result from the interaction between the vorticity and the rate of strain tensor, do not depend only on the magnitude of ω2\omega^{2} and s2s^{2}, but also on the geometry of the velocity derivatives. For all cases 𝝎\bm{\omega} is mostly aligned with e^β\hat{e}_{\beta}, similarly to HIT. This feature has been often associated with the mechanism of axial stretching of vortices (ashurst-etal-1987). Figure 19 shows that the first eigenvector 𝒆^α\hat{\bm{e}}_{\alpha} shows very little correlation with 𝝎^\hat{\bm{\omega}} (in particular at large G​aGa), as indicated by the almost flat distribution, while the last eigenvector is mostly perpendicular to the vorticity, with a peak at |𝝎^⋅𝒆^γ|=0|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\gamma}|=0. The influence of G​aGa is nonmonotonous and changes with the eigenvector. For G​a≤450Ga\leq 450, an increase of G​aGa promotes events with |𝝎^⋅𝒆^α|=0|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\alpha}|=0, |𝝎^⋅𝒆^β|=1|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\beta}|=1 and |𝝎^⋅𝒆^γ|=0|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\gamma}|=0: the vorticity aligns more strongly with the intermediate eigenvector 𝒆^β\hat{\bm{e}}_{\beta} and becomes more perpendicular to the first and last eigenvectors 𝒆^α\hat{\bm{e}}_{\alpha} and 𝒆^γ\hat{\bm{e}}_{\gamma}. At these parameters, the nonlinear terms are subdominant and the fluid-phase fluctuations are mostly associated with the shear layers generated at the surface of the fibres, which become stronger as G​aGa increases. In a purely shear flow, indeed, the compressional and extensional directions are in the plane of the shear flow, while the vorticity is aligned with the out-of-plane direction, leading thus to |𝝎^⋅𝒆^α|=0|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\alpha}|=0, |𝝎^⋅𝒆^β|=1|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\beta}|=1 and |𝝎^⋅𝒆^γ|=0|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\gamma}|=0. However, when further increasing G​aGa (Ga>450)Ga>450) the opposite occurs and events with |𝝎^⋅𝒆^α|=1|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\alpha}|=1, |𝝎^⋅𝒆^β|=0|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\beta}|=0 and |𝝎^⋅𝒆^γ|=1|\hat{\bm{\omega}}\cdot\hat{\bm{e}}_{\gamma}|=1 are promoted, i.e. vorticity aligns more with the first and last eigenvectors and less with the second. For G​a≥450Ga\geq 450 the mechanisms associated with the nonlinear terms become relevant, and the geometry of the velocity derivative field changes accordingly.

The top panels of figure 20 show that the two distributions are right-skewed, with both positive and negative tails becoming longer as G​aGa increases. The mean value ⟨𝒫ω2⟩\langle\mathcal{P}_{\omega^{2}}\rangle (and ⟨𝒫s2⟩\langle\mathcal{P}_{s^{2}}\rangle) increases more than linearly with G​aGa; see the inset in figure 20(b)(b). This is consistent with the enhanced nonlinear energy transfer rate discussed in §4.3. Interestingly, figure 20(b)(b) demonstrates that the distributions of 𝒫ω2\mathcal{P}_{\omega^{2}} and 𝒫s2\mathcal{P}_{s^{2}} collapse nicely for all G​aGa, once the quantities are normalised with their mean value.

We find that for all G​aGa the largest contribution comes from the first eigenvalue, like in HIT (ashurst-etal-1987); see table 3. Indeed, although figure 19 shows a strong alignment between 𝝎\bm{\omega} and 𝒆^β\hat{\bm{e}}_{\beta}, β\beta can be both positive and negative, while α\alpha is always positive and generally much larger (see figure 17). By looking at the influence of G​aGa, we find that the relative contribution of 𝒫ω2β\mathcal{P}_{\omega^{2}}^{\beta} increases with G​aGa, while that of 𝒫ω2α\mathcal{P}_{\omega^{2}}^{\alpha} and 𝒫ω2γ\mathcal{P}_{\omega^{2}}^{\gamma} decreases and then increases. This nonmonotonous trend is inherited by the nonmonotonous dependence of the vorticity-eigenvector alignment on G​aGa (see figure 19 and the related discussion).

5.3 Invariants of ai​ja_{ij}

We close this section by looking at the invariants of the velocity gradient tensor ai​ja_{ij} (davidson-2004). A second-order tensor possesses three invariants, PP, QQ and RR, which are directly related to its eigenvalues λ\lambda by the characteristic polynomial function

λ3+P​λ2+Q​λ+R=0,\lambda^{3}+P\lambda^{2}+Q\lambda+R=0,

(25)

where

P=α+β+γ,Q=−12​(α2+β2+γ2)+ω24​and​R=−13​𝒫s2−13​𝒫ω2.\begin{gathered}P=\alpha+\beta+\gamma,\ Q=-\frac{1}{2}\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+\gamma^{2}\right)+\frac{\omega^{2}}{4}\ \text{and}\ R=-\frac{1}{3}\mathcal{P}_{s^{2}}-\frac{1}{3}\mathcal{P}_{\omega^{2}}.\end{gathered}

(26)

Here, P=0P=0 is due to the incompressibility constraint. The second invariant QQ provides a measure of the relative intensity of the strain and vorticity, with large negative QQ indicating regions of strong strain and large positive QQ regions of intense vorticity. The third invariant RR, instead, is a measure of the relative intensity of the production of vorticity/enstrophy, i.e. vortex stretching (R<0)(R<0) and production of strain/dissipation (R>0R>0). The discriminant of the characteristic equation, i.e. Δ=27​R2/4+Q3\Delta=27R^{2}/4+Q^{3}, is used to distinguish between regions where the motion is mainly vortical (Δ<0\Delta<0) and regions with a node-saddle streamline pattern (Δ>0\Delta>0). When QQ is large and positive, the strain is locally weak and R∼−𝒫ω2R\sim-\mathcal{P}_{\omega^{2}}. In this case, R<0R<0 implies vortex stretching, while R>0R>0 implies vortex compression. When, instead, QQ is large and negative, then R∼𝒫s2=−α​β​γR\sim\mathcal{P}_{s^{2}}=-\alpha\beta\gamma. Here a negative RR implies a region of axial strain (α>0\alpha>0, β<0\beta<0 and γ<0\gamma<0), while a positive RR a region of biaxial strain (α>0\alpha>0, β>0\beta>0 and γ<0\gamma<0).

In HIT the shape of the Q−RQ-R JPDF takes a tear-drop pattern, with a clear point at the right-Vieillefosse tail with Δ=0\Delta=0, R>0R>0 and Q<0Q<0 (meneveau-2011). The distribution of QQ is skewed towards negative values, and this is particularly true for the smaller G​aGa. The probability of events with negative QQ and small RR is relatively large, generating a second cluster close to the left Vieillefosse tail. A similar phenomenon was observed for heavy Kolmogorov-size particles in HIT and related to the deformation of the fluid in the proximity of the particles (chiarini-tandurella-rosti-2025). The left panels of figure 22 show that for smaller G​aGa the probability of events with Q>0Q>0 and R<0R<0 decreases, indicating a weakening of the vortex stretching events in agreement with the above discussion.

(27)

are represented separately. These invariants are related to the fluid dissipation ε=−4​ν​QS\varepsilon=-4\nu Q_{S} and with the fluid enstrophy ω2=4​QW\omega^{2}=4Q_{W}. Although the kinematic (i.e., it does not descend from the Navier–Stokes equations) relation ⟨ε⟩=ν​⟨ω2⟩\langle\varepsilon\rangle=\nu\langle\omega^{2}\rangle holds, these two quantities are dynamically different and there is not a causal relationship between them. The QS−QWQ_{S}-Q_{W} JPDF shows whether the flow is dominated by fluid dissipation (extensional dominated regions with QS>QWQ_{S}>Q_{W}) or by enstrophy (rigid rotation regions with QW>QSQ_{W}>Q_{S}). In HIT, events with QW>−QSQ_{W}>-Q_{S} are more frequent meaning that the flow is mainly dominated by rigid rotations (meneveau-2011). In the present case, the scenario is rather different. Indeed, events having large −QS-Q_{S} and small QWQ_{W} are promoted as well as events with −QS≈QW-Q_{S}\approx Q_{W}. The last effect is particularly prominent for the smaller G​aGa (G​a≤450Ga\leq 450) and is characteristic of shear-dominated flows (soria-etal-1994). This further confirms that, while settling, the fibres induce a chaotic motion of the fluid phase which (at least in the descending flow region) is mainly extensional dominated and characterised by a two-dimensional state of straining, consistently with the shear layers that generate at the fibres surface.

6 Conclusions

We have investigated the fluid-solid interaction of suspensions of rigid fibres settling in a quiescent fluid using DNS coupled with an IBM. The main focus of the study is on the dynamics and organisation of the fluid-phase velocity fluctuations. Specifically, the objectives are: (i) to examine the role of the Galileo number and the fibre concentration on the scale-by-scale redistribution of fluid-phase kinetic energy; (ii) to characterise the flow anisotropy across scales; (iii) to elucidate the mechanisms sustaining fluid-velocity fluctuations; and (iv) to investigate the local structure of the fluid-phase velocity field under different parameter regimes.
We considered heavy fibres with a solid-to-fluid density ratio of ρf/ρ=𝒪​(100)\rho_{f}/\rho=\mathcal{O}(100) and explored a broad range of the parameter space by varying the Galileo number in the range G​a∈[180,900]Ga\in[180,900] and the concentration in the range n​ℓf3∈[0.36,23.15]n\ell_{f}^{3}\in[0.36,23.15].

The settling fibres generate a chaotic fluid motion with properties that vary as a function of G​aGa and n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}, and depend on the spatial organisation of the solid phase. At higher G​aGa and lower n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}, the fibres cluster and form streamers. At lower G​aGa and/or higher n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}, the fibres show a more homogeneous distribution and a lower level of clustering. At the present parameters, the fibres mostly settle aligned with the direction of gravity.

The fluid-phase kinetic energy increases with G​aGa across the entire parameter space considered, and the complete energy spectrum ℰ3​c\mathcal{E}_{3c} shows that the excited range of scales broadens accordingly. At large G​aGa and/or small n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}, the relative amount of energy contained at the small scales (κ⪆κℓ\kappa\gtrapprox\kappa_{\ell}) increases, while that at the larger scales (κ⪅κℓ\kappa\lessapprox\kappa_{\ell}) decreases, due to the so-called spectral shortcut (brandt-coletti-2022). The anisotropy of the flow across scales is analysed by separately considering the out-of-plane (ℰ1​c\mathcal{E}_{1c}) and in-plane (ℰ2​c\mathcal{E}_{2c}) contributions to the total energy spectrum in the full (κ⟂,κ∥)(\kappa_{\perp},\kappa_{\parallel}) wavenumber space. We observe that motions with in-plane length scales larger than those in the vertical direction (κ∥<κ⟂\kappa_{\parallel}<\kappa_{\perp}) are dominated by uu and vv fluctuations, whereas vertically elongated motions (κ∥>κ⟂\kappa_{\parallel}>\kappa_{\perp}) are primarily associated with ww fluctuations.

Scale-by-scale energy budgets in Fourier space reveal that the out-of-plane (ℰ1​c\mathcal{E}_{1c}) and in-plane (ℰ2​c\mathcal{E}_{2c}) velocity fluctuations are sustained by distinct physical mechanisms. The vertical fluctuations are primarily driven by the fluid-solid coupling term, ℱ1​c\mathcal{F}_{1c}, which acts as a source for ℰ1​c\mathcal{E}_{1c} across all scales. In contrast, the pressure-strain term, 𝒫2​c\mathcal{P}_{2c}, acts to energise the in-plane components by redistributing energy from the vertical direction, thereby promoting isotropy in the flow. This term serves as a sink in the ℰ1​c\mathcal{E}_{1c} budget and a corresponding source in the ℰ2​c\mathcal{E}_{2c} budget, consistently across all scales.
In addition, the fluid-solid coupling term in the in-plane budget, ℱ2​c\mathcal{F}_{2c}, exhibits a scale-dependent behaviour: it extracts energy from the large scales (κ<κℓ\kappa<\kappa_{\ell}) and injects it into the small scales (κ>κℓ\kappa>\kappa_{\ell}). The influence of nonlinear transfer becomes increasingly prominent with G​aGa, in line with the growing role of fluid inertia. At high G​aGa and/or low fibre concentrations (n​ℓf3n\ell_{f}^{3}), this results in an average energy transfer from larger to smaller scales.
However, by employing the Kármán–Howarth–Monin–Hill (KHMH) equation (hill-2002), which enables a rigorous definition of the scale-by-scale energy cascade rate, we also identify intense localised backscatter events. These correspond to regions where energy is transferred from smaller to larger scales and are present at both small and large G​aGa.

The influence of the governing parameters on the local structure of the fluctuating velocity field has been analysed through the velocity gradient tensor ai​j=∂ui/∂xja_{ij}=\partial u_{i}/\partial x_{j} and its invariants (meneveau-2011). At low G​aGa, the flow is dominated by axisymmetric compression and two-dimensional straining motions, typically associated with shear layers forming at the fibre surfaces. In contrast, increasing G​aGa, the local strain state becomes largely spatially dependent due to the presence of streamers. In particular, descending regions (characterised by a high local fibre concentration) remain dominated by two-dimensional strain states, similar to the low-G​aGa regime, whereas ascending regions (where the fibre concentration is lower) are dominated by axisymmetric extensions, more reminiscent of homogeneous isotropic turbulence.
The joint probability density function of the second and third invariants of ai​ja_{ij} reveals that events involving axial strain and vortex compression occur with significantly higher frequency than in HIT (ashurst-etal-1987). In agreement with the enhanced nonlinear energy transfer, at higher G​aGa the vortex stretching and strain self-amplification mechanisms are amplified, leading to increased production of both vorticity/enstrophy and strain/dissipation. Finally, an inspection of the JPDF of the second invariants of the symmetric and antisymmetric parts of ai​ja_{ij} indicates that extensional events are more prevalent than rotational ones, highlighting the dominant strain-driven nature of the fluctuations in these flows.

These findings open several avenues for future investigation. In particular, it would be valuable to examine how the scale-by-scale organisation of fluid velocity fluctuations is modified by the presence of flexible fibres with varying lengths and bending stiffnesses. Fibre geometry and flexibility are known to influence their settling configurations (banaei-etal-2020; marchioli-rosti-verhille-2025), and flexible fibres, in particular, interact strongly with fluid motions at comparable scales (rosti-etal-2018). Another promising direction is the study of fibre settling in non-Newtonian fluids, which have recently been shown to alter settling behaviour significantly (alghalibi-etal-2020). Understanding these effects would extend the current framework and offer further insight into the complex dynamics of fibre-laden flows.
